exemplar.title: Investigating the effect of contextual information on the recall of details of a passage by Greek adolescents: a partial replication of Bransford and Johnson (1972) study.

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the effect of contextual information on the recall of details of a passage by Greek adolescents: a partial replication of Bransford and Johnson (1972) study.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim is precisely stated and relevance to schema activation and educational practice is clearly explained.
Schema theory and original study are accurately described and explicitly connected to the replication design.
Both null and research hypotheses include correctly identified and fully operationalized IV and DV.
weaknesses: Practical relevance paragraph ends abruptly and could further elaborate direct implications for instructional design.
Justification for focusing on only two conditions in the replication could be more explicitly linked to study scope.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the top-band descriptors: the aim and relevance are explained, the theory/model is described and linked to the study, and hypotheses are fully operationalized. Minor elaboration points do not detract from overall excellence.

strengths: Research design is explained with rationale for reduced demand characteristics in an independent-samples design.
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are described and the choice is justified in terms of language fluency and group balance.
Controlled variables and materials are not only listed but each control is explained in relation to internal validity.
weaknesses: Random allocation method is outlined but lacks depth on how it minimizes selection bias.
Materials list could further justify why each instrument (e.g., stopwatch) is critical for measurement reliability.
Potential researcher bias in key-idea unit selection is noted without interrater reliability data.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand achieves the âexplainedâ level (band 3-4). The controls, sampling, and design are well justified, with only minor gaps in depth, meriting the top of the middle band (4).

strengths: Appropriate use of means, SDs, and a MannâWhitney U test is correctly calculated and interpreted.
Graphical presentation (bar chart) is accurate, clearly labeled, and directly addresses the hypothesis.
Findings are interpreted in the context of data and linked back to the hypothesis regarding schema activation.
weaknesses: Minor mis-labelling of data level and slight confusion in wording p-value versus test statistic.
Normality test results are not specified to justify the non-parametric choice more rigorously.
Interpretation could more explicitly connect back to schema-theory predictions in this section.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands meet top-band criteria for appropriate and accurate statistical and graphical analysis with clear interpretation. Small technical and wording issues justify a score of 5 rather than full marks.

strengths: Findings are discussed relative to the original study and schema theory, noting reasons for non-significance.
Strengths and limitations are clearly explained and directly relevant to validity and generalizability.
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and justified in terms of improving the study design.
weaknesses: Discussion of findings lacks alternative theoretical explanations and broader educational implications.
Conclusion restates the null finding but could expand on directions for future research or practice.
Depth of discussion is moderate, placing it below the highest band despite strong evaluation of strengths and modifications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands achieve top-band (strengths/limitations, modifications) and one sits in the mid band (findings discussion). The overall moderate depth across all three strands supports a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart with labelled axes and title directly compares mean recall for each condition, clearly addressing the hypothesis.  No plotting or labelling errors are evident, fulfilling top-band criteria.

The student states that the difference was non-significant, links this to retention of the null hypothesis, and explicitly interprets what this means for schema activation.  Interpretation is firmly grounded in the statistical outcome and data pattern.

Both null and research hypotheses are provided.  The IV (presence vs absence of contextual image) and DV (number of keyâidea units out of 14 recalled) are not only identified but fully operationalised, meeting the top-band requirement.

Opportunity sampling is identified and justified (time-saving).  Participant characteristics, numbers, gender balance and language proficiency are explained, indicating why these choices mattered (English fluency, non-psychology students).  This moves beyond bare description.

Schema theory is accurately described (organisation of prior knowledge guiding encoding/retrieval).  The original Bransford & Johnson study is summarised and explicitly connected to the present replication, showing an understanding of how the theory underpins the current IVâDV relationship.

Findings are discussed relative to Bransford & Johnson and schema theory, noting inconsistency.  While the discussion is present, depth is moderate and alternative theoretical explanations are limited, placing it in the mid-band rather than the highest.

The student states a clear aim â to investigate whether a contextual image improves recall of key ideas â and explicitly explains why this matters for understanding schema activation and for classroom practice.  The relevance is not merely identified but explicitly linked to potential educational applications, demonstrating purpose beyond mere restatement of the original study.

Multiple strengths (English fluency, standardisation) and limitations (participant variability, opportunity sample, engagement with picture) are not only stated but clearly explained with relevance to validity and generalisability, satisfying the top descriptor.

Controlled variables (standardised instructions, fixed recall time, same location/time) are given with rationale, and all materials are listed with brief justification.  The student explains why each control helps validity, satisfying the âexplainedâ level.

Suggested modifications (matched-pairs design, random sampling, longer image exposure) are each explicitly tied to the corresponding limitation and justified in terms of how they would improve validity or generalisability, fulfilling the highest band requirements.

The independent-samples design is described and its main strength (reduced demand characteristics) is explained, but limitations are only briefly touched on later under evaluation.  The explanation is adequate but not developed in great depth, fitting the middle band.

Means, standard deviations and an appropriate Mann-Whitney U test for independent groups with small, non-normal data are used.  Calculations, critical value comparison and p-value interpretation are all correct; only a minor mis-labelling of data level is present, so the strand meets the âappropriate and accurateâ description.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear description of schema theory, explaining how mental frameworks govern encoding and retrieval, demonstrating solid understanding of theoretical foundations.
The summary of Bransford & Johnsonâs design is informative but could explicitly justify focusing only on two conditions in the replication to clarify its scope.
The conclusion restates the null finding but could be expanded with broader educational implications or future research directions.
The matched-pairs design modification is well linked to the individual differences limitation; consider also counterbalancing order effects as an alternative.
Key idea unit selection may introduce researcher bias; reporting interrater reliability for unit identification would strengthen materials validity.
The aim is precisely stated and operationalised, linking the independent (context picture) and dependent (keyâidea recall) variables clearly.
The paragraph on practical relevance ends abruptly and would benefit from elaborating how these findings could directly inform instructional design.
Including both null and research hypotheses with correctly identified and operationalised variables meets the topâband requirement.
The independentâsamples design is described clearly, and the rationale for reduced demand characteristics is well explained.
Control variables (standardized instructions, fixed time, pilot tests) are identified with clear justification for how they enhance internal validity.
Participant sampling by opportunity is described with characteristics; stating group size, gender balance, and English fluency supports transparency of the sampling process.
Random allocation via the hat method is outlined; elaborating how this procedure reduces selection bias would strengthen justification.
The materials list is comprehensive; consider elaborating why each instrument (e.g., stopwatch) is critical for reliable measurement.
The procedural steps are laid out clearly in sequence, supporting replicability and transparency of the method.
Referencing the raw data appendix is helpful, but including a brief interpretation of means and SD here would reinforce reader understanding.
Table 1 presents the descriptive statistics with clear labels directly addressing the hypothesis.
The bar chart is well constructed with titles and axes labeled, effectively addressing the research hypothesis.
Specify the outcome of any normality tests performed to justify the choice of the MannâWhitney U test more rigorously.
The statement "p-value found was bigger that 0.05" confuses p-value and U value language; refine wording to accurately distinguish test statistic from significance level.
Link the inferential outcome explicitly back to schemaâtheory predictions in this section to strengthen interpretation coherence.
The discussion situates non-significant results clearly in relation to the original study and schema theory.
The limitation regarding participant variability is described with direct relevance to internal validity.
Consider discussing alternative theoretical explanations for the non-significance (e.g., age-related schema development) to deepen the evaluation.
Noting the English fluency strength and proposing random sampling to improve generalizability demonstrates relevant evaluation of external validity.
The procedural engagement limitation (variable attention to the image) is explained and a modification is suggested, linking limitation to improvement.
